subject: maths
title: Areas Related to Circles

nodes.title: Find the area of a segment of a circle
nodes.brief: Compute the area of a segment as the area of the corresponding sector minus the area of the triangle formed by the two radii and the chord.
nodes.quote: Area of the segment APB = Area of the sector OAPB – Area of Δ OAB
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Decomposing the segment.
A segment is the region enclosed between a chord AB and the arc it cuts off. It equals the sector OAPB (formed by the two radii and the arc) minus the triangle OAB (formed by the two radii and the chord): Segment = Sector − Triangle.

PART 2 — Computing the sector area.
Use Area(sector) = theta/360 × pi*r². For a segment with central angle theta and radius r, substitute and simplify. For r = 14 cm, theta = 60°: sector = 60/360 × 22/7 × 196 = 1/6 × 616 = 102.67 cm².

PART 3 — Computing the triangle area and subtracting.
For an equilateral triangle (theta = 60°), all sides = r = 14, so Area(triangle) = sqrt(3)/4 × 14² = sqrt(3)/4 × 196 = 49*sqrt(3) ≈ 84.87 cm². Segment = 102.67 − 84.87 = 17.80 cm² ≈ 17.89 cm² (small rounding differences from pi and sqrt(3) approximations).
keyMoves: Identify the sector and the triangle that make up the segment
Compute area of the sector with θ/360 × πr²
misconceptions: Adding instead of subtracting the triangle area
Confusing the minor segment with the major segment
Forgetting that the triangle area must be computed separately, not assumed equal to the sector

nodes.title: Apply the sector-area formula θ/360 × πr²
nodes.brief: Compute the area of a sector of angle θ and radius r using Area = θ/360 × πr².
nodes.quote: Area of the sector of angle θ = θ/360 × πr²,
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Setting up the fraction.
The sector angle θ out of the full 360° gives the fraction θ/360. This is the portion of the full circle that the sector occupies. For example, a 90° sector covers 90/360 = 1/4 of the circle.

PART 2 — Multiplying by the circle area.
The full circle area is pi*r². Multiply by the fraction: Area of sector = θ/360 × pi*r². For r = 7 cm and θ = 90°: Area = 90/360 × 22/7 × 49 = 1/4 × 154 = 38.5 cm².

PART 3 — Substituting and evaluating.
Replace θ, r, and pi with the given values and simplify step by step. Cancel where possible before multiplying — e.g., 22/7 × 49 cancels to 22 × 7 = 154 before dividing by 4.
keyMoves: Take the fraction θ/360 of the full angle
Multiply by the circle area πr²
Substitute the given θ, r, π and evaluate
misconceptions: Using θ/180 or θ/720 instead of θ/360
Using 2πr (circumference) in place of πr²
Forgetting to square the radius

nodes.title: Derive sector area by the Unitary Method
nodes.brief: Reason that a 360° angle gives area πr², so 1° gives πr²/360, hence θ° gives θ/360 × πr².
nodes.quote: So, when the degree measure of the angle at the centre is 1, area of the sector = πr²/360
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Starting from the full circle.
A complete circle subtends 360° at its centre and has area pi*r². This is the anchor fact: a 360° sector IS the whole circle, so its area equals pi*r². Every other sector is a fraction of this.

PART 2 — Scaling down to one degree.
Divide the full-circle area by 360 to find the area for a 1° sector: area for 1° = pi*r²/360. This is the unitary step — finding the value of one unit.

PART 3 — Scaling up to θ degrees.
Multiply the one-degree area by θ to get the area of a θ° sector: Area = θ/360 × pi*r². So a 90° sector has area 90/360 × pi*r² = pi*r²/4, exactly one-quarter of the circle.
keyMoves: Start from: 360° corresponds to area πr²
Divide by 360 to get area for 1°
Multiply by θ to get area for θ°
misconceptions: Multiplying instead of dividing when scaling to one degree
Treating the relationship as non-proportional

nodes.title: Compute the length of an arc of a sector
nodes.brief: Find arc length as θ/360 of the full circumference 2πr.
nodes.quote: So, length of an arc of a sector of angle θ = θ/360 × 2πr
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Starting from the full circumference.
The circumference of a complete circle is 2*pi*r. A full circle spans 360°, so the full circumference corresponds to 360°.

PART 2 — Taking the fraction for arc length.
An arc of θ° is the fraction θ/360 of the full circumference: Arc length = θ/360 × 2*pi*r. For r = 7 cm and θ = 90°: Arc = 90/360 × 2 × 22/7 × 7 = 1/4 × 44 = 11 cm.

PART 3 — Applying to perimeter of a sector.
The perimeter of a sector = arc length + 2r (the two radii). For r = 7, θ = 90°: perimeter = 11 + 14 = 25 cm.
misconceptions: Using πr² (area) instead of 2πr (circumference)
Dropping the factor of 2 in 2πr

nodes.title: Compute the area of the major sector
nodes.brief: Find the major sector area either as πr² minus the minor sector, or with angle (360 − θ).
nodes.quote: Area of the major sector OAQB = πr² – Area of the minor sector OAPB
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Understanding major and minor sectors.
When a central angle θ (less than 180°) creates a minor sector, the remaining region is the major sector with angle (360 − θ). Minor sector + major sector = full circle.

PART 2 — Subtracting from the full circle.
Major sector area = pi*r² − minor sector area. For r = 7, θ = 60°: minor sector = 60/360 × 22/7 × 49 = 25.67 cm²; major sector = 154 − 25.67 = 128.33 cm².

PART 3 — Using the complement angle directly.
Alternatively, plug (360 − θ) into the sector formula: major sector area = (360 − θ)/360 × pi*r². For θ = 60°, r = 7: major area = 300/360 × 22/7 × 49 = 5/6 × 154 = 128.33 cm². Both methods must give the same result.
keyMoves: Or use angle (360 − θ) in the sector formula
misconceptions: Using θ instead of (360 − θ) for the major sector
Forgetting that minor + major sector = whole circle

nodes.title: Compute triangle area as ½ × base × height
nodes.brief: Find the area of triangle OAB using ½ × AB × OM, where OM is the perpendicular height.
nodes.quote: So, area of Δ OAB = 1/2 AB × OM = 1/2 × 21√3 × 21/2 cm²
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identifying base and height in triangle OAB.
The chord AB is the base of triangle OAB. The perpendicular from O to AB is OM, the height. These are the two measurements needed for the area formula.

PART 2 — Applying the area formula.
Area = ½ × base × height = ½ × AB × OM. For AB = 21*sqrt(3) cm and OM = 10.5 cm: Area = ½ × 21*sqrt(3) × 10.5 = ½ × 21 × 10.5 × sqrt(3) = 441*sqrt(3)/4 cm² ≈ 190.95 cm².

PART 3 — Simplifying the result.
Factor where possible: ½ × 21*sqrt(3) × 10.5 = ½ × 21 × (21/2) × sqrt(3) = 441*sqrt(3)/4 cm². In decimal: 441 × 1.732 / 4 = 763.812/4 = 190.95 cm².
keyMoves: Identify the base AB and the height OM
Substitute the found lengths and simplify
misconceptions: Using a slant side OA as the height instead of the perpendicular OM
Forgetting the factor of ½

nodes.title: Drop a perpendicular from centre to chord
nodes.brief: Draw OM ⊥ AB; by RHS congruence M is the midpoint of AB and OM bisects the central angle.
nodes.quote: For finding the area of Δ OAB, draw OM ⊥ AB as shown in Fig. 11.7.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Drawing OM perpendicular to AB.
Construct the perpendicular from centre O to chord AB, meeting AB at M. This is the key construction move that unlocks the triangle's area computation by creating right angles at M.

PART 2 — Recognising the isosceles triangle.
Since OA = OB (both radii), triangle OAB is isosceles. The perpendicular from the apex O to the base AB of an isosceles triangle always bisects the base — so M is the midpoint of AB.

PART 3 — Concluding the bisection of the central angle.
By RHS congruence (ΔAMO ≅ ΔBMO), the perpendicular also bisects the central angle ∠AOB. If ∠AOB = 120°, then ∠AOM = ∠BOM = 60°. Now the two resulting right triangles are ready for trig-ratio work.
keyMoves: Construct OM perpendicular to chord AB
Recognise OA = OB so the triangle is isosceles
Conclude M bisects AB and OM bisects the central angle
misconceptions: Assuming M is the midpoint without justifying it
Not drawing the perpendicular and trying to use OA as height

nodes.title: Use RHS congruence to halve the central angle
nodes.brief: By RHS, ΔAMO ≅ ΔBMO, so ∠AOM = ∠BOM = ½ of the central angle.
nodes.quote: Note that OA = OB. Therefore, by RHS congruence, Δ AMO ≅ Δ BMO.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identifying the two right triangles.
When OM is drawn perpendicular to chord AB, point M lies on AB with angle OMA = angle OMB = 90°. This creates two triangles, ΔAMO and ΔBMO, each with a right angle at M.

PART 2 — Applying RHS congruence.
Both triangles share the side OM (common), have equal hypotenuses OA = OB (both radii), and right angles at M. By the RHS criterion (Right angle – Hypotenuse – Side), ΔAMO ≅ ΔBMO.

PART 3 — Concluding both bisections.
From the congruence: AM = BM (so M bisects chord AB) and angle AOM = angle BOM (so OM bisects the central angle). If the central angle was 2α, each half is α. For example, a 120° central angle gives angle AOM = angle BOM = 60°.
keyMoves: Note right angles at M and common side OM
Conclude the central angle is halved
misconceptions: Using SSS or ASA when RHS is the correct criterion
Failing to use OA = OB

nodes.title: Use cos 60° to find the perpendicular OM
nodes.brief: From right triangle OMA, OM/OA = cos 60° = ½, giving OM = r/2.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Setting up cos in right triangle OMA.
After halving the central angle, triangle OMA has a right angle at M. The angle at O is half the central angle (e.g., half of 120° = 60°). The side adjacent to angle O is OM, and the hypotenuse is OA = r.

PART 2 — Applying cos 60° = 1/2.
cos(angle AOM) = adjacent/hypotenuse = OM/OA. So OM = OA × cos(60°) = r × 1/2 = r/2. For r = 21 cm: OM = 21/2 = 10.5 cm.

PART 3 — Reading off the result.
OM is the perpendicular height from the centre to the chord. For any central angle 2α, OM = r × cos(α). This value is used as the height in the triangle-area formula.
misconceptions: Using sin instead of cos for the adjacent side
Misremembering cos 60° as √3/2

nodes.title: Use sin 60° to find half-chord AM
nodes.brief: From right triangle OMA, AM/OA = sin 60° = √3/2, giving AM = (r√3)/2.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Setting up sin in right triangle OMA.
In right triangle OMA (right angle at M), the angle at O is the half-angle (e.g., 60° when central angle is 120°). The side opposite to angle O is AM, and the hypotenuse is OA = r.

PART 2 — Applying sin 60° = sqrt(3)/2.
sin(angle AOM) = opposite/hypotenuse = AM/OA. So AM = OA × sin(60°) = r × sqrt(3)/2. For r = 21 cm: AM = 21 × sqrt(3)/2 = 21*sqrt(3)/2 ≈ 18.19 cm.

PART 3 — Reading off AM for later use.
AM is the half-chord. It will be doubled to get the full chord AB = 2 × AM = 21*sqrt(3) cm, which becomes the base of the triangle OAB.
misconceptions: Using cos instead of sin for the opposite side
Misremembering sin 60° as 1/2

nodes.title: Find chord length AB = 2 × AM
nodes.brief: Since the perpendicular bisects the chord, the full chord AB is twice the half-chord AM.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognising M as the midpoint.
The perpendicular from the centre to a chord always bisects the chord (proven by RHS congruence). So if M is the foot of the perpendicular, then AM = MB and AB = 2 × AM.

PART 2 — Doubling AM to find AB.
Substitute the value of AM found from the sine ratio. For AM = 10.5*sqrt(3) cm: AB = 2 × 10.5*sqrt(3) = 21*sqrt(3) cm ≈ 36.37 cm. This length is the base of triangle OAB.

PART 3 — Using AB in the area formula.
Once AB is known, it serves as the base in Area(OAB) = ½ × AB × OM. With AB = 21*sqrt(3) and OM = 10.5: Area = ½ × 21*sqrt(3) × 10.5 = 441*sqrt(3)/4 ≈ 190.95 cm².
keyMoves: Recognise M is the midpoint of AB
Multiply AM by 2 to get AB
misconceptions: Forgetting to double AM and using AM as the chord
Doubling OM instead of AM

nodes.title: Subtract triangle from sector to get the segment
nodes.brief: Combine the computed sector area and triangle area: segment = sector − triangle.
nodes.quote: Therefore, area of the segment AYB = ( 462 – 441/4 √3 ) cm²     [From (1), (2) and (3)]
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Assembling the two computed quantities.
From earlier steps: sector OAPB has area 462 cm² (for r=21, θ=120°); triangle OAB has area 441*sqrt(3)/4 cm² ≈ 190.95 cm².

PART 2 — Subtracting to get the segment.
Segment area = Sector area − Triangle area = 462 − 441*sqrt(3)/4 cm². Numerically: 462 − 190.95 = 271.05 cm².

PART 3 — Verifying the subtraction direction.
The segment is the region between the chord and the arc — it is SMALLER than the sector (which also includes the triangle). So Segment < Sector, confirming subtraction gives a positive result. Never add: that would give a region larger than the sector.
keyMoves: Bring together the sector area and triangle area
misconceptions: Subtracting in the wrong order (triangle − sector)
Adding the two areas

nodes.title: Substitute the given value of π and evaluate
nodes.brief: Substitute π = 22/7 or π = 3.14 as instructed, then carry out the arithmetic.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Reading the prescribed pi value.
Every problem specifies which approximation to use: pi = 22/7 when the radius is a multiple of 7, and pi = 3.14 otherwise. Read the problem carefully — using the wrong approximation gives a different numerical answer, even if the algebra is correct.

PART 2 — Substituting and evaluating.
Replace pi with its given value in the formula, then compute step by step. For example, if Area = pi × 7², substitute pi = 22/7: Area = 22/7 × 49 = 22 × 7 = 154 cm². Cancel factors before multiplying to keep the arithmetic manageable.
keyMoves: Read which π value the problem prescribes
misconceptions: Using a different π value than the one specified
Arithmetic slips with fractions like 22/7

narrative:
## What was read

I re-read the full segmented chapter `jemh111.txt` (NCERT Class 10 Maths, "Areas Related to Circles") — SEG 0 (the 11.1 exposition + Examples 1 and 2), SEG 1 (Exercise 11.1), SEG 2 (Summary) — and the shared `_maths-exams.txt` marking schemes (CBSE Standard SQP Q30 A/B and Basic SQP Q34) for answer-keyed gate material.

## Correctness audit results

The draft's node graph, grain, and edges were sound and I kept them. The teachable content lives entirely in SEG 0 (three formulae + two worked examples); SEG 1/SEG 2 only restate. Per D-117 the pure exposition (sector/segment definitions, minor/major naming, Remark, history) is dropped; Example 2 is unrolled into the failable chain: drop OM ⊥ AB → RHS congruence to halve the angle → cos 60° → OM → sin 60° → AM → double to AB → ½·base·height → triangle area → sector − triangle → segment. Each is one independently-failable skill (D-107) and is a node.

## Citations (D-120) — re-verified every quote verbatim

I located every node quote and every gate quote as a contiguous character-for-character substring of its tagged segment (chapter sec 0, exam MS lines). All 14 node quotes verify in SEG 0. Whitespace was confirmed (e.g. the five spaces before "(3)" in `= 441/4 √3 cm²     (3)`; the math-italic theta glyph 𝜃 and ∘ in `⇒ 𝜃 = 60∘`).

## Gate fixes applied

1. **Goal gate (Q30B) quote was wrong.** The draft quoted `= 69.23 cm2`, which (a) does not match the answer 17.89 and (b) is itself a marking-scheme ERROR: that MS row computes with angle 90° (`= 90/360 × 22/7 × 14×14 − √3/4 ×14×14`) even though the problem states ∠AOB = 60°. Solving from first principles for the stated 60°: sector = 60/360 × 22/7 × 196 = 102.67 cm²; the triangle is equilateral so area = √3/4 × 14² = 1.73/4 × 196 = 84.77 cm²; minor segment = 102.67 − 84.77 ≈ 17.89 cm². The correct value 17.89 IS printed verbatim in the MS margin (line 530), so I kept answer = 17.89 and replaced the quote with the verbatim `17.89`. Tol 0.5 absorbs rounding.

2. **compute-major-sector-area (alternative-method gate).** Draft answer 46.1 did not match its quoted line `= 330/360 × 3.14 × 16 cm² = 46.05 cm²` (which yields 46.05). I set the answer to 46.05 to match the quote exactly (46.05 → rounds to the chapter's 46.1; both are correct, but the gate now agrees with its own cited line). The other major-sector gate keeps 46.1 with its `= 46.05 cm² = 46.1 cm² (approx.)` quote.

I independently re-solved every other gate and all were correct: 4.19 (12.56/3), θ=60 (Q34: 48 + 0.4187θ = 73.12), 190.95 (½·21√3·10.5), ∠AOM=60, RHS=C, OM=10.5, AM=18.19, AB=36.37, segment=271.05 (462−190.95), 594 (210/360 × 22/7 × 324). The Q14 MCQ answer D (`p/720 × 2πR²`) is algebraically `p/360 × πR²` = the correct sector-area form, so D is right.

## Edges

Single root `find-area-of-segment-of-circle`; acyclic; no orphans; every endpoint is a defined slug. Segment goal requires sector-area, triangle-area, and the subtraction; triangle-area requires perpendicular-drop, chord-length, cosine-ratio; these bottom out at RHS-congruence (bedrock). Arc-length and major-sector hang off the goal as parallel chapter skills sharing the same numeracy bedrock.

## Honestly absent / uncertain

- No composite/combination-of-plane-figures worked example exists in this transcription (SEG 0–2 only) — not authored.
- The Q30B marking scheme is internally inconsistent (uses 90° not the stated 60°); I corrected to the mathematically right 17.89, citing the chapter segment skill and the verbatim 17.89 in the MS. This is the gate to watch downstream.
- Some quotes retain font-substituted glyphs (𝜃, 𝑐𝑚2) copied exactly as in the source, not normalised, so the exact-substring grader matches.
absentNotes:
A fuller treatment of "Areas Related to Circles" (and adjacent mensuration) would teach several things this transcription's chapter does NOT, so they are intentionally omitted (not failed):
- Areas of COMBINATIONS of plane figures (circle inscribed in/around a square, triangle, or polygon; flower-bed/design composite areas) — no such worked example appears in SEG 0–2 of this transcription.
- Perimeter of a sector as an explicit taught formula (2r + arc). It appears only in the EXAM source (Basic Q34), not stated in the chapter body, so it is captured via the arc-length node + an exam gate rather than as its own taught node.
- Area between two concentric circles / annulus (ring) area.
- Circumference and area of a full circle as derivations — assumed prior knowledge ("You know that area of a circle ... is πr²"), used but not taught here, hence not a node.
- Conversion of the angle described by a clock hand into degrees as a separately-taught skill — used in exercises/exam but not demonstrated in a chapter worked example.
- Radian measure of angles — not part of Class 10 / not in this chapter.
- Distinguishing major vs minor SEGMENT areas by subtraction from the whole circle is stated only in a Note ("Area of major segment AQB = πr² – Area of the minor segment APB"); the failable computation is folded into compute-major-sector-area rather than duplicated as a near-identical node.
